main_summary.summary: Dion Diamond, interviewed by David Cline on December 13, 2015, recounts his trajectory from Petersburg, Virginia, where he conducted spontaneous solo sit-ins at five-and-dime lunch counters as a teenager, through Howard University's Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), the Freedom Rides, SNCC field organizing in Mississippi and Louisiana, and post-Movement careers at the U.S. Department of Labor, in economic-development training, and at HUD and HEW. Diamond entered Howard in September 1959 as a physics major. After the Greensboro sit-ins, NAG desegregated the Drug Fair lunch counter in Arlington, Virginia, within two weeks and picketed Glen Echo Park through the summer of 1960. He joined the Freedom Rides after the Anniston bus burning, was arrested in Jackson on a breach-of-peace charge, and moved through the Hinds County jail, prison farm, and Parchman Farm, where he was cellmate to James Farmer. Reassigned to SNCC voter-registration work in Pike County, Mississippi, around McComb, he shared a house with Robert "Bob" Parris Moses and survived a shotgun blast through the bedroom window. Later arrested in Baton Rouge on a criminal-anarchy charge for organizing a Southern University boycott, he served solitary at the East Baton Rouge Parish jail. He transferred to the University of Wisconsin in September 1963, later attended Harvard's graduate school of education, and worked in housing, anti-poverty, and Medicaid/Medicare fraud-detection consulting.
key_themes: Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) at Howard University
Freedom Rides and Parchman Farm incarceration
SNCC voter registration in Pike County, Mississippi
Nonviolence as tactic rather than philosophy
main_summary.historical_significance:
Diamond's testimony documents the Washington, D.C. movement infrastructure that operated parallel to better-known Nashville and Atlanta organizing, specifically the Nonviolent Action Group at Howard, which desegregated Arlington's Drug Fair counters within two weeks of Greensboro and sustained the Glen Echo Park picket line through the summer of 1960. His firsthand account of the cell-by-cell movement of Freedom Riders from the Hinds County jail through the prison farm to Parchman provides primary-source detail on the scale of the arrests, including his recollection that he shared a Parchman cell with CORE director James Farmer and later a McComb safe house with Robert "Bob" Parris Moses.

Diamond's articulation of nonviolence as tactic rather than philosophy or religion, grounded in the practical observation that "the police and the army have the guns", is a counter-voice to the canonical Lawson/King framing and reflects the diversity of theoretical positions within SNCC. His account of the Robert F. Kennedy–Mississippi governor agreement to arrest rather than permit beatings of Riders, and of Cold War media pressure as the operative leverage, contributes to the historiography of federal-state negotiation during the Rides.

chapters.title: Sit-Ins Before They Had a Name
chapters.summary: Introduced by David Cline in December 2015, Diamond traces his first eighteen years to a totally segregated Petersburg, Virginia, where he staged haphazard, solitary protests before the sit-in had a name, refusing to leave the five-and-dime lunch counter until the police came and venturing past the colored entrance into the library stacks.

chapters.title: A Summer in the Catskills
chapters.summary: At sixteen Diamond left home for Monticello, New York, in the Catskills, and when the laundry job fell through he bluffed his way into a short-order cook position he had no experience for, phoning a friend to vouch for a fabricated reference; the integrated resort town was his first real exposure to white people.

chapters.part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
chapters.title: The Nonviolent Action Group
chapters.summary: Enrolling at Howard University in 1959 as a physics major, Diamond found the campus doing nothing about segregation until Greensboro, when he and others formed the Nonviolent Action Group and, because the District itself was not segregated by law, crossed into Virginia to sit in at a segregated lunch counter.

chapters.part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
chapters.title: The Drug Fair and the Nazi Party
chapters.summary: Diamond recounts the group's first sit-in at the Drug Fair lunch counter in Cherrydale, Arlington, desegregating the area within two weeks, and the day George Lincoln Rockwell and his American Nazi Party surrounded the demonstrators, a danger his youthful exuberance kept him from fully registering.

chapters.part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
chapters.title: Nonviolence as a Tactic
chapters.summary: Looking at the old photographs of his own calm, Diamond explains his lasting position that nonviolence was a tactic, not a philosophy or creed: the police and the army held the guns and the demonstrators had nothing, so violence made no sense, though off-camera he would gladly have fought back.

chapters.part: Howard and the Nonviolent Action Group
chapters.title: The Glen Echo Picket
chapters.summary: After the Virginia victory the group turned to Glen Echo Park, a Maryland amusement park closed to people of color, picketing it through the summer of 1960; Diamond recalls the stunt arranged with labor figure Hyman Bookbinder, arriving by black limousine in an African gown with a white chauffeur and interpreter to test the gate in broken French.

chapters.title: Into Jackson
chapters.summary: When the Freedom Rides nearly collapsed after the Anniston bus burning, Diamond was among those who continued the ride from Montgomery to Jackson under a heavy National Guard, state-police, and helicopter escort; only later did he learn of the deal between Robert Kennedy and Mississippi's governor that spared them a beating but guaranteed their arrest for breach of peace.

chapters.title: Performing for the Cameras
chapters.summary: Diamond describes the press as the riders' salvation and his own conscious restraint for the cameras, knowing a single thrown punch would cost them their goodwill; he had no formal nonviolence training, only the instinct that a fight he was outnumbered in could not be won, and recalls a Jet photograph that wrongly seemed to show him throwing a punch.

chapters.title: Running Out of Space
chapters.summary: Arrested the moment he stepped off the bus, Diamond was held in the Hinds County jail, then moved to the county prison farm and the state penitentiary, the prisoners segregated by race and sex; it was weeks before he understood that the constant transfers were because the jails kept running out of space as more buses arrived.

chapters.title: A Cell With James Farmer
chapters.summary: On the penitentiary's death row Diamond shared a cell with CORE director James Farmer and later a McComb house with Bob Moses, and describes being humbled into silence by both, his own vociferous leadership met with monosyllables; he marvels that Moses turned out to have relatives back in Petersburg.

chapters.title: Parents Who Learned From Reporters
chapters.summary: Diamond reflects on a lifelong independence, leaving home at seventeen and holding office from class president onward, that meant he never asked permission for what he did; like other young activists, his parents learned of his arrests only when a reporter called, proud of his involvement but wishing it had been someone else's child.

chapters.title: Into SNCC at McComb
chapters.summary: Having lost the semester to Parchman, Diamond flew to Atlanta and took his first SNCC assignment on the voter-registration project in Pike County around McComb, Mississippi; connections made through his Washington work and a Nashville leadership seminar had already linked him to the movement's network, bonds that have lasted half a century.

chapters.title: What We Unleashed
chapters.summary: Recalling that there was not one Black officer or elected official when he was arrested, Diamond describes returning for the fiftieth-anniversary reunion to a Black mayor of Jackson and a Black prison warden, change he never imagined; he closes on the inscribed warning that what is past is prologue and his belief that education is the surest way to keep the gains.

chapters.title: No Long-Range Plan
chapters.summary: Asked whether his involvement at nineteen felt like a choice or a necessity, Diamond says he had no long-range plan and could not have defined what he was contributing to, only that he wanted to break down the barriers of segregation; Mississippi in 1961, he found, was worse than Petersburg and far more fearful.

chapters.title: A Shotgun Through the Window
chapters.summary: Diamond describes the McComb house he shared with Bob Moses, run by an independent woman they called Mama Cotton, where his bed sat beneath a lattice window; one night a shotgun blast came straight through it and passed over his slender body, making the danger of the work suddenly and permanently real.

chapters.title: Charged With Overthrowing Louisiana
chapters.summary: Sent from McComb to Holly Springs for voter registration, Diamond crossed to Baton Rouge to recruit students whom Southern University president Felton Clark had expelled over sit-ins; when Clark reopened the school and wrecked the plan, Diamond organized a class boycott to reinstate the leaders and found himself, a stranger, leading a protest on a campus that had never heard of him.

chapters.title: Solitary Confinement
chapters.summary: Charged with criminal anarchy for trying to overthrow the state, Diamond was jailed in the East Baton Rouge general population alongside men awaiting transfer to Angola, gaining their respect as a freedom rider even as the guards offered them time off for roughing him up; an NAACP lawyer reached the FBI and he was moved to a solitary cell, where he learned he could endure isolation and told the time only by his meals.

chapters.title: Thirty Arrests
chapters.summary: Diamond recounts voter-registration work on Maryland's eastern shore alongside Gloria Richardson, where local families took him in, and tallies roughly thirty arrests in connection with civil rights activity, more than he can recall, telling job applications to check with the FBI for the dates and locations.

chapters.title: The Wisconsin Transfer
chapters.summary: Raising funds for SNCC at the 1963 National Student Association Congress in Columbus, Diamond realized his Howard class was graduating while he had been in the field for two years, and a chance remark to the University of Wisconsin's student-government president led, within days, to a transcript and enrollment; he switched from physics to history and sociology to ask why organized protest had taken a century to come.

chapters.title: The Harvard Offer
chapters.summary: After anti-poverty work in Washington with the United Planning Organization, Diamond was asked in 1968 by a colleague at the federal Office of Economic Opportunity whether he would consider Harvard; he treated it as a silly question and went, studying race relations and crediting both his grades and the era's push to diversify the student body.

chapters.title: The Only One in the Room
chapters.summary: At both Wisconsin and Harvard Diamond was often the only Black student in the room, deferred to by professors to verify or refute on behalf of all people of color, a role he found uncomfortable but assumed; his one regret was forming no lasting friendships there, unlike at Howard, leaving Harvard with a degree in education and a thesis on race and ethnic relations.

chapters.part: What Was Lost, What Was Gained
chapters.title: The Stokely Carmichael Story
chapters.summary: Asked about a famous jailing with Stokely Carmichael, Diamond gently corrects the record, placing it at the Mississippi penitentiary rather than Nashville, and says he can neither confirm nor deny Carmichael's published account of him taunting an armed guard to shoot; he allows that he was capable of it, knowing a guard would never kill in front of a cellblock of witnesses.

chapters.part: What Was Lost, What Was Gained
chapters.title: The Stolen Geometry Test
chapters.summary: Diamond tells, without pride, how he learned his high IQ in high school and, terrified of a standardized geometry exam, used a baseball tossed onto a shop roof as a ruse to steal the test, only to ace it without the answers; the episode taught him he had badly underrated the education and the attentive teachers of his segregated school.

chapters.part: What Was Lost, What Was Gained
chapters.title: The Bittersweet of Desegregation
chapters.summary: Diamond calls desegregation bittersweet: teachers in the small segregated community knew his parents and took a deeper interest than large modern schools allow, the close binding friendships have thinned, and the historically Black colleges have been robbed of their most gifted athletes by the big recruiting institutions.

chapters.part: What Was Lost, What Was Gained
chapters.title: The Glass Ceiling
chapters.topic: One or Two at the Top
chapters.summary: He describes a glass ceiling for any person of color, pointing to a Black CEO of American Express or Holland America as exceptions that mask how little the rest of the infrastructure has changed, and puzzles at a society that prizes athletes and entertainers over the educated; raised by a mail-carrier father and a homemaker mother, he credits an early-instilled horror of ending up broke.

chapters.title: Chocolate City
chapters.summary: Diamond credits Marion Barry, the former SNCC chairman turned mayor, with bringing so many movement veterans into Washington government jobs in what was then called Chocolate City; the old relationships endure without much socializing, drawn together mainly now by the deaths of comrades like Julian Bond and Barry himself.

chapters.title: A Career in Reform
chapters.summary: Diamond sketches a winding career in reform: a high federal grade at the Labor Department secured through a Glen Echo connection, training African Americans in economic development, and then his own consulting firm advising HUD and HEW on segregated housing, race relations, and detecting fraud in Medicaid and Medicare.

chapters.title: Creating Wealth, Not Just a Job
chapters.summary: Semi-retired since the 2008 collapse, Diamond traces the through-line from civil rights to a life of reform he would never have led as the physicist he trained to be, and argues that the next hurdle is economic: too many people seek the security of a job rather than the harder work of creating wealth, a lesson that turned him fiscally conservative.

chapters.title: A Self-Described Recluse
chapters.summary: Diamond admits disappointment in himself for becoming a recluse who no longer advances civil rights or mentors the young, partly because he cannot relate to their culture; he believes the work should be generational, and voices ambivalence about Black Lives Matter, fearing the framing segments an issue he would rather see as a shared human relationship.

chapters.title: Trump Rallies and Ellis Island
chapters.topic: How Far There Is to Go
chapters.summary: Pointing to the Charleston church shooting and the crowds at Donald Trump rallies that struck him as almost a Klan gathering, Diamond warns again that what is past is prologue, that a country could revert and forget the Ellis Island on which it was built; well-traveled, he is glad to have been born in America yet certain there is a long way still to go.

chapters.title: A Hell of a Mosaic
chapters.summary: Diamond names his most memorable moment as the 1964 Civil Rights Act and the Black officials it made possible, and recalls the 1968 crossroads of a Marshall Islands anti-poverty post, the Robert Kennedy campaign, and Harvard, ending at Harvard only because Kennedy was killed; he calls the Civil Rights Act and Obama's election the highlights, noting it took a Southerner in Lyndon Johnson to deliver, all of it rooted in the movement.

loc_video.caption: Dion Diamond oral history interview conducted by David Cline in Washington, District of Columbia, 2015 December 13
